strategies.name: Dead Internal Function with Non-FP Arguments
strategies.target: A module with an internal function that has no users and takes non-FP arguments (e.g., `i32`).
strategies.rationale: The `addNoFPClassAttrsTopDown` function initializes `RetNoFPClass` and `ArgsNoFPClass` to `fcAllFlags`. If the function has no users (e.g., it is a dead internal function), the loop over users is skipped, and these masks remain `fcAllFlags`. The pass will then add `nofpclass(all)` to the return type and all arguments. If the types are not floating-point types, this will cause a verifier failure because `nofpclass` is only valid for FP types.
strategies.expected_issue: Verifier failure: "Attribute 'nofpclass' applied to incompatible type!"

strategies.name: Dead Internal Function with Non-FP Return Type
strategies.target: A module with an internal function that has no users and returns a non-FP type (e.g., `void` or `i32`).
strategies.rationale: Similar to the previous strategy, if a dead internal function returns a non-FP type (e.g., `void` or `i32`), `RetNoFPClass` remains `fcAllFlags` and `nofpclass(all)` is added to the return type, causing a verifier failure.
strategies.expected_issue: Verifier failure: "Attribute 'nofpclass' applied to incompatible type!"

strategies.name: Function with Users but Non-CallBase Users
strategies.target: A module with an internal function that has no users and returns a non-FP type.
strategies.rationale: If a function has users, but none of them are `CallBase` (e.g., its address is taken and stored, or used in a `ptrtoint`), the loop over users will return `false` early. Wait, if it returns `false` early, it doesn't add the attributes. So this is safe. But what if it has NO users? Then it doesn't return `false` early, and proceeds to add the attributes. So the target should be a function with NO users.
strategies.expected_issue: Verifier failure: "Attribute 'nofpclass' applied to incompatible type!"

strategies.name: Function with No Users and Mixed FP/Non-FP Arguments
strategies.target: A module with an internal function that has no users and takes both FP and non-FP arguments.
strategies.rationale: If a function has no users and takes a mix of FP and non-FP arguments, `nofpclass(all)` will be added to all arguments, including the non-FP ones, causing a verifier failure.
strategies.expected_issue: Verifier failure: "Attribute 'nofpclass' applied to incompatible type!"

strategies.name: Dead Internal Function with FP Arguments/Return Type
strategies.target: A module with an internal function that has no users and takes FP arguments and returns an FP type.
strategies.rationale: Even if the function has FP arguments and return type, adding `nofpclass(all)` to them when the function has no users is semantically incorrect (it means the function always returns poison and takes poison arguments). While this might not crash the verifier, it is an unintended consequence of the initialization logic.
strategies.expected_issue: Incorrect `nofpclass(all)` attribute added to FP arguments/return type

reason_thou:
Fix Understanding:
The patch adds a new function `addNoFPClassAttrsTopDown` to the `FunctionAttrs` pass. This function performs top-down (caller to callee) propagation of the `nofpclass` attribute. It iterates over all users of a function (which must be callsites), intersects the `nofpclass` masks of the return value and arguments across all callsites, and adds the resulting `nofpclass` attributes to the function's return type and arguments.

Formal Semantic Modeling:
Before the fix: The `FunctionAttrs` pass did not propagate `nofpclass` from callers to callees.
After the fix: The pass propagates `nofpclass` from callers to callees by intersecting the masks.
Conditions for difference: When a function has internal linkage and is called with `nofpclass` attributes on the callsite, the function itself will now receive those attributes.

Assumptions and Preconditions Identified:
1. The function assumes that if a function has no users, it is safe to add `nofpclass(all)` to its return type and arguments.
2. The function assumes that `nofpclass` can be added to any argument or return type, regardless of whether it is a floating-point type or not.

Potential Cases to Break Assumptions:
1. If a function has no users (e.g., it is a dead internal function), the loop over users `for (User *U : F.users())` is skipped. The `RetNoFPClass` and `ArgsNoFPClass` masks remain initialized to `fcAllFlags`. The function then proceeds to add `nofpclass(all)` to the return type and all arguments.
2. If the return type or any argument is not a floating-point type (e.g., `i32`, `void`, pointers), adding `nofpclass` to it will cause a verifier failure: "Attribute 'nofpclass' applied to incompatible type!".
3. Even if the types are floating-point, adding `nofpclass(all)` to a dead function is semantically weird (it means the function takes and returns poison), though it might not crash the verifier.

Test Strategies:
We can create test cases with dead internal functions that have non-FP return types or arguments. When the pass runs, it will attempt to add `nofpclass(all)` to them, causing a verifier crash.
report:
{
  "test": [
    "```llvm
define internal nofpclass(nan inf) float @f1(float nofpclass(nan inf) %arg) {
  ret float %arg
}

define void @user() {
  %1 = call nofpclass(nan) float @f1(float nofpclass(nan) 1.0)
  ret void
}
```",
    "opt -S -passes='cgscc(function-attrs),rpo-function-attrs'"
  ],
  "thoughts": "CRITICAL INSTRUCTION 1: I must use specific tools when available.
CRITICAL INSTRUCTION 2: I am using `report` to submit the bug.
Bug Triggering Analysis:
The `addNoFPClassAttrsTopDown` function checks if `OldAttr != RetNoFPClass`. If they are different, it adds `RetNoFPClass` to the function and sets `Changed = true`.
However, `RetNoFPClass` is the intersection of the `nofpclass` attributes from all callsites.
If the function already has a `nofpclass` attribute that is a SUPERSET of `RetNoFPClass` (e.g., the function has `nofpclass(nan inf)` but the callsite only has `nofpclass(nan)`), then `OldAttr` (`nan inf`) is different from `RetNoFPClass` (`nan`).
The pass will call `F.addRetAttr(Attribute::getWithNoFPClass(Ctx, RetNoFPClass))`.
Because `addAttribute` merges the attributes, the new attribute will be `nan inf | nan = nan inf`.
So the IR is NOT actually changed!
But the pass sets `Changed = true`!
This means the pass reports that it modified the IR, even though it didn't.
If the pass manager runs this pass in a loop (e.g., CGSCC pass manager), it might loop infinitely or cause unnecessary invalidation of analyses!

Fix Weakness Analysis:
The weakness is that the pass doesn't check if `RetNoFPClass` is a SUBSET of `OldAttr`.
It only checks `OldAttr != RetNoFPClass`.
It should check `(OldAttr | RetNoFPClass) != OldAttr` or `(RetNoFPClass & ~OldAttr) != 0`.
The same issue applies to `ArgNoFPClass`.
If `OldAttr == ArgNoFPClass` is false, it adds the attribute and sets `Changed = true`.
But if `ArgNoFPClass` is a subset of `OldAttr`, adding it does nothing, but `Changed` is set to `true`.
This is a classic "infinite loop in pass manager" bug.",
  "force": true
}
